title: Tea on Silk
timeEstimate: Pro-lane within hours; cold-water back-flush only at home
stainChemistry: Tea is theaflavins + thearubigins (oxidized catechin polymers) + tannin polyphenols. Black tea stains harder than green (more oxidation). Tannins bond to silk fibroin via hydrogen bonding to amino acid sites — same chemistry as wine tannin on silk. Silk's banned-tools list applies: no enzymes (digest fibroin), no hot water (denatures fiber + sets tannin), no OxiClean / alkaline (weakens silk + DARKENS tannin), no peroxide (yellows silk dyes), no scrubbing (ruptures wet silk). Tea-with-milk adds casein protein, which adds protein-before-heat ordering on top of all this. Home protocol = cold-water back-flush, then pro.
whyThisWorks: A silk specialist with cold acetic acid + sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach, silk-safe) can lift fresh tea tannin without damaging silk. Home chemistry on silk for tea has too many ways to go wrong: vinegar over-dwell weakens fiber, peroxide yellows silk, OxiClean both weakens silk AND locks tannin into brown.
defaultAssumption: Silk + tea = pro within hours. Black tea is the tougher variant; tea-with-milk is even tougher (casein adds protein step). Home is cold-water blot only.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from reverse, into catch towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water through the BACK of the stain over a white catch towel. Press, no scrubbing.
Diluted acetic acid via dropper. Press cloth, no rubbing. Strict 60-sec ceiling. Flush cold immediately.
For oxidized brown only. Reducing bleach is silk-safer than peroxide. Strict 60-sec dwell.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD, cold water, dry white towels
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD flush + cold rinse until clear. Press between dry towels, lay flat, never hang.

steps: 1. Cold water back-flush at first contact.
3. Sodium hydrosulfite for oxidized residue, 60-sec ceiling.
warnings: NEVER hot water — denatures silk + sets tannin
NEVER OxiClean / alkaline — weakens silk + DARKENS tannin
NEVER hydrogen peroxide on silk — yellows dyes

homeSolutions.instruction: Tea on silk is a pro-lane case. The tools that work on cotton (OxiClean, hot water, peroxide) all damage silk — and several of them DARKEN the tannin permanently while damaging the fiber. Home protocol = cold-water back-flush to extract bulk tea, get to a silk specialist within hours.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Back-Flush — Pour Through, Not On Top
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn inside out. Place a white towel under the stain. Run COLD water (ice cold preferred) through the BACK of the stain into the catch towel. Cold ONLY — heat denatures silk and sets tea tannin. The water runs THROUGH the silk into the towel, never pooled on top. 60 seconds usually pulls fresh tea to a pale shadow.

homeSolutions.agent: Press Dry — Don't Apply Anything Else
homeSolutions.instruction: Press silk between two dry white towels to lift moisture. No wringing. Lay flat at room temperature, out of sun. Do NOT apply OxiClean, peroxide, vinegar, baking soda, or any cleaner. Silk's best outcome is reaching the pro with nothing applied.

CALL FIRST — "Do you treat silk with acetic acid + sodium hydrosulfite for tannin?" If they don't understand, try another cleaner. Tell them: "Tea on silk, [black / green / herbal / with milk], age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No acid, no peroxide, no detergent applied." The black-vs-green and milk-vs-no-milk details matter for the pro plan.

steps: 2. Cold water back-flush from the reverse, into a catch towel.
3. Press dry between white towels — no other chemistry.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after the cold-water back-flush. Anything else home-applied risks worse damage than the tea.
warnings: NEVER OxiClean / alkaline — DARKENS tannin permanently
NEVER scrub or wring wet silk
Pour through, not on top — water rings

neverDo: Never wring or scrub wet silk
fiberSensitivities: Silk is protein fiber — same enzyme-digestion risk as wool

materialWarnings: Pro within hours — home is damage-prevention only
Alkaline cleaners darken tannin AND weaken silk
Cold back-flush is the entire home protocol

escalation.when: Always — silk + tea is pro-lane, regardless of stain age.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tea on silk, [black / green / herbal / with milk], age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No acid, no peroxide, no detergent applied."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in silk (call ahead — confirm acetic acid + sodium hydrosulfite spotting)

contextQuestions: Black tea, green tea, herbal, or chai (with milk)?
How long ago — minutes, hours, or older?
Type of silk — charmeuse / chiffon / habotai / dupioni?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black tea harder than green (more oxidation). Tea with milk adds casein — different ordering for pro. Thinner silks more vulnerable. Pre-applied OxiClean = tannin likely darkened permanently.
